claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin the assessment, I need to know about the child (or children) you'd like to claim Child Benefit for.

**How old is the child you want to claim for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please let me know their ages too, and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I've got two children actually - Alex who's 16, and Blake who's just 5 years old. I wanted to see if I can claim for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! It's lovely to meet you, and I'm happy to help you work out if you can claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let's go through the assessment for each child. Since they're different ages, the requirements may differ slightly.

---

**Let's start with Blake (age 5):**

Since Blake is under 16 years old, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next step for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Blake (age 5), I need to ask:

**Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

(For a 5-year-old, the answer is almost certainly yes - they wouldn't be in paid employment - but I need to confirm this as part of the assessment.)

---

Once we've confirmed this for Blake, we'll then look at **Alex (age 16)**, where the requirements will be a bit different since they're in the 16-19 age bracket.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, Blake's only 5, so no, they're definitely not working at all! They're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I thought! Blake is definitely not working, so that's confirmed. Let me continue the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question for Blake:

**Is Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by the child themselves. (Again, for a 5-year-old, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't get any benefits in their own name - they're only 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes complete sense! Let me continue with Blake's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question for Blake:

**Is Blake in an apprenticeship?**

(Again, at age 5 and in primary school, I'm confident the answer is no - but just to confirm!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're just 5 and in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! Blake is clearly not in an apprenticeship. Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. This applies to you as the claimant (for both Blake and Alex).

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **one** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK**
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **Have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. **Have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. **Living abroad as a Crown servant** or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to your situation? (For most people, option 1 - normally living in the UK - is the most common.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward! You normally live in the UK, so you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your responsibility for the children. This applies to both Blake and Alex.

**Are you responsible for Blake and Alex?**

You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the child** (most common)
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care, such as food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that covers the child's upkeep

Do either or both children live with you, or do you contribute to their care in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - they live with their mum. I do help out financially with Alex though - I give money towards their clothes and school things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that - it's really helpful to know the different situations for each child.

So we have:
- **Blake** - lives with you ✓
- **Alex** - doesn't live with you, but you contribute financially towards their care

For Alex, since you contribute towards their clothes and school things, that can count as contributing to their upkeep. However, I need to check one thing:

**For Alex, do your financial contributions equal at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment?**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The current Child Benefit rates vary (you can check the exact amounts on gov.uk), but roughly speaking:

**Do you contribute at least around £25 per week (or equivalent monthly/as lump sums) towards Alex's upkeep?** This includes things like money for clothes, school supplies, food, pocket money, presents, or housing costs.

It doesn't have to be cash - it can also be buying things directly for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I give £50 a week towards their upkeep - for clothes, school dinners, things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - £50 a week definitely meets the requirement for contributing to Alex's upkeep. So you are considered responsible for both children:

- **Blake** - lives with you ✓
- **Alex** - you contribute to their upkeep ✓

Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question, especially since Alex lives with their mum.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake or Alex?**

- For **Blake** - is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for them?
- For **Alex** - is their mum (or anyone else) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is already claiming for Alex, we'll need to work out who has priority or if you've agreed between yourselves who should claim.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me and nobody else is claiming for them. But for Alex - yes, their mum already claims Child Benefit for them, and Alex lives with her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - this is really helpful information.

**For Blake** - no other claimant, so we can continue with the assessment. ✓

**For Alex** - this is a more complex situation. Since Alex's mum already claims Child Benefit for Alex *and* Alex lives with her, she would generally have priority for the claim. The rule is that the person the child **lives with** takes priority over someone who contributes financially.

Let me ask: **Have you and Alex's mum agreed that you should be the one to claim Child Benefit for Alex instead of her?** Or would you like to continue claiming for them even though their mum currently receives it?

If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision - but typically, the person the child lives with has the stronger claim.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we haven't agreed that I should claim it - she's the one claiming it and Alex lives with her, so I suppose that's how it should be.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes sense, and it sounds like you've got a good understanding of the situation. Since Alex lives with their mum and she's already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, she has priority for that claim.

So let's focus on **Blake** for your Child Benefit claim. Let me continue the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Blake's living situation.

**Where is Blake currently living?**

- **At home with you** (normal living situation)
- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **In a foster care arrangement**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives at home with me - just a normal living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Nearly there! One final question for Blake:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, you're not receiving any local council payments specifically for Blake's housing or upkeep? (This is just to confirm that Blake isn't in a council-supported arrangement.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Blake. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That completes the assessment for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! I've now completed the assessment for both children. Let me give you the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete!

Here's the summary for your two children:

### ✅ Blake (age 5) - **ELIGIBLE**
You **can** claim Child Benefit for Blake. They meet all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Lives with you
- Not in paid work or receiving benefits
- No one else is claiming for them
- Council isn't paying towards their care

### ❌ Alex (age 16) - **NOT ELIGIBLE for you to claim**
You **cannot** claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex lives with their mum
- Their mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The person the child lives with has priority for the claim

Your £50/week contribution towards Alex is wonderful support, but it doesn't override the priority given to the parent Alex lives with.

---

## Next Steps for Blake

To claim Child Benefit for Blake, you should:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Blake
3. **Provide Blake's birth certificate**
4. **Be aware**: If you or a partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receive certain benefits)

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

reasonings: Blake (age 5) meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: (1) They are under 16 years old, so they automatically qualify on age. (2) They are not working. (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right. (4) They are not in an apprenticeship. (5) You, the claimant, live in the UK (Manchester). (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. (8) Blake lives at home in a normal living situation. (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All criteria are satisfied.

Alex (age 16) - You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because: (1) Alex lives with their mum, not with you. (2) Their mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (3) Under the rules, when two people could claim for the same child, the person the child lives with has priority. Since Alex lives with their mum and she is already claiming, she has the rightful claim. You have not agreed between yourselves to transfer the claim to you. Your financial contributions (£50/week) towards Alex's upkeep are commendable, but they do not override the priority given to the person Alex lives with.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with you and meets all eligibility requirements. However, you cannot claim for Alex because Alex lives with their mum, who already claims Child Benefit for them - and the person the child lives with has priority for the claim.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Blake (age 5) meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: (1) They are under 16 years old, so they automatically qualify on age. (2) They are not working. (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right. (4) They are not in an apprenticeship. (5) You, the claimant, live in the UK (Manchester). (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. (8) Blake lives at home in a normal living situation. (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All criteria are satisfied.

Alex (age 16) - You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because: (1) Alex lives with their mum, not with you. (2) Their mum is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (3) Under the rules, when two people could claim for the same child, the person the child lives with has priority. Since Alex lives with their mum and she is already claiming, she has the rightful claim. You have not agreed between yourselves to transfer the claim to you. Your financial contributions (£50/week) towards Alex's upkeep are commendable, but they do not override the priority given to the person Alex lives with.

response.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with you and meets all eligibility requirements. However, you cannot claim for Alex because Alex lives with their mum, who already claims Child Benefit for them - and the person the child lives with has priority for the claim.
